perturbation.original_text: 5.5 Governing Law This Agreement shall be governed by and inte rpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its choice of laws rules. The parties hereby agree that any dispute regarding the interpretation or valid ity of, or otherwise arising out of, this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive ju risdiction of the California state courts of Santa Clara County, California (or, if there is fe deral jurisdiction, the Un ited States District Court for the Northern District of California, San Jose), and the parties agree to submit to the personal and exclus ive jurisdiction and ve nue of these courts.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.3 No Agency The parties hereto are independent parties, and nothing herein shall be construed to create an agency, joint venture, partnership or ot her form of business a ssociation between the parties hereto.

5.5 Governing Law This Agreement shall be governed by and inte rpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its choice of laws rules. The parties hereby agree that any dispute regarding the interpretation or valid ity of, or otherwise arising out of, this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive ju risdiction of the Delaware state courts of Kent County, Delaware (or, if there is fe deral jurisdiction, the Un ited States District Court for the District of Delaware), and the parties agree to submit to the personal and exclus ive jurisdiction and ve nue of these courts.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause has been moved to a less prominent location and the state was changed from California to Delaware, and the county from Santa Clara to Kent county. This change could confuse readers and make it more difficult to understand which jurisdiction's laws apply, conflicting with the original intent of clear governance.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the jurisdiction to Delaware and Kent County, the agreement may violate 28 U.S.C. § 1391 if the participant's business operations and relevant activities are primarily located in California, and the cause of action arises there. The statute dictates that venue should be proper in a judicial district where any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the state in which the district is located; a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law and jurisdiction from California, Santa Clara County, to Delaware, Kent County. The Law Explanation and scraped law snippets suggest this change may violate 28 U.S.C. § 1391, which addresses venue. Specifically, 28 U.S.C. § 1391 (b)(1) states a civil action may be brought in a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located. If the defendant's business operations and the cause of action are substantially connected to California, changing the venue to Delaware could contradict this statute if it is not where the defendant resides or where the events giving rise to the claim occurred as described in (b)(2). This constitutes a structural flaw where the altered clause regarding governing law conflicts with the statute governing proper venue, making it harder to enforce in certain situations.

perturbation.original_text: 5.7 No Warranty ALL PARTIES ACKNOWLEDGE THAT ALL INFORM ATION PROVIDED UNDE R THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING ANY SPECIFICATIONS AND CONTRIBU TIONS, ARE PROVIDED “AS IS” WITH NO WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS WHATSOEVER, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, STATUTORY, OR OTHERWISE, AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSL Y DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OF MERCHANTABILITY, SECURITY, SATIS FACTORY QUALITY, NONINFRINGEMENT INCLUDING NON-INFRINGEMENT OF THIRD PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, FITNESS FOR ANY PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ERROR-FREE OPERATION, OR ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OTHERWISE ARISING OUT OF ANY PROPOSAL, CONTRIBUTION, SPECIFICATION, OR SAMPLE.
perturbation.changed_text:
4.2 Return of Confidential Information Upon termination, both parties will destroy or return to the other party all tangible copies of Confidential Information th at are in its possession.

5.7 No Warranty ALL PARTIES ACKNOWLEDGE THAT ALL INFORM ATION PROVIDED UNDE R THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING ANY SPECIFICATIONS AND CONTRIBU TIONS, ARE PROVIDED “AS IS” WITH NO WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS WHATSOEVER, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, STATUTORY, OR OTHERWISE, AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSL Y DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OF MERCHANTABILITY, SECURITY, SATIS FACTORY QUALITY, FITNESS FOR ANY PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ERROR-FREE OPERATION, OR ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OTHERWISE ARISING OUT OF ANY PROPOSAL, CONTRIBUTION, SPECIFICATION, OR SAMPLE.
perturbation.explanation: The 'non-infringement' clause has been removed from the 'No Warranty' section. While an 'as is' warranty can generally disclaim all warranties, the removal of specific protections against intellectual property infringement could now be a point of legal contention. This relocation reduces the clarity and enforceability of the IP protection aspects.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-312. Warranty Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the explicit mention of 'non-infringement' warranty, the altered text may be interpreted as weakening the Participant's protection against claims that the provided information infringes on third-party intellectual property rights. This contradicts UCC § 2-312, which implies a warranty against infringement unless explicitly disclaimed by circumstances or language. Although an 'as is' statement can be construed as a disclaimer, removing the specific clause can render the disclaimer ambiguous in specific circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. (1) Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that (a) the title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (b) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is purporting to sell only such right or title as he or a third person may have. (3) Unless otherwise agreed a seller who is a merchant regularly dealing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the specifications. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit 'non-infringement' clause from the 'No Warranty' section, potentially weakening protection against intellectual property infringement claims. The Law Explanation suggests this could contradict UCC § 2-312, which implies a warranty against infringement unless explicitly disclaimed. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-312, states that a merchant regularly dealing in goods warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement. While the original text had a disclaimer, removing 'non-infringement' specifically introduces ambiguity about whether infringement is disclaimed. This removal creates a structural flaw by making the contract less clear on IP protection, potentially undermining legal enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Confidentiality Obligation Both parties will maintain all Confidential Information in confidence with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its ow n confidential and propri etary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care unde r the circumstances and will not disclose the Confidential Information except as necessary for its authorized participants to evaluate and comment thereon. Both parties will mark any copies it makes of such Confidential Information “Strictly Confidential,” or with a similar legend and shall reproduce all copyright notices and disclaime rs contained in the Confidential Information. Unless the parties agree otherwise, this obligation of confidentiality will expire two (2) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.6 Complete Agreement This Agreement constitute the complete and exclusive statement of the agreement between the parties, and supersedes all previous agr eements, proposals, oral or written, and all other communications or understandings between the parties relating to the subject matter of this Agreement. Each party acknowledges that it has not relied up on any representation or statement not contained herein. This Agr eement shall not be modified, amended or in any way altered except by an instrument in writing signed by the Participant and Khronos hereto.

4.1 Confidentiality Obligation Both parties will maintain all Confidential Information in confidence with the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information and will not disclose the Confidential Information except as necessary for its authorized participants to evaluate and comment thereon. Unless the parties agree otherwise, this obligation of confidentiality will expire ten (10) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The 'reasonable degree of care under the circumstances' clause has been removed, and the expiration from 2 years to 10 years. This reduces the explicit requirement for a reasonable standard of care and significantly extends the confidentiality period. This can be seen as unenforceable and therefore a flaw.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the 'reasonable degree of care' clause and changing the confidentiality period to 10 years, the modification could be seen as creating an unreasonable or unconscionable term, especially if the information becomes obsolete or publicly available over time. This may contradict Cal. Civ. Code § 1643, which states that a contract must be interpreted to give it effect, implying terms should be reasonable and not lead to absurd results. The original clause ensures enforceability by setting a reasonable standard, while the modification increases the possibility of rendering the clause unenforceable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1643. A contract must receive such an interpretation as will make it lawful, operative, definite, reasonable, and capable of being carried into effect, if it can be done without violating the intention of the parties. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1643. The original text included a 'reasonable degree of care' standard for maintaining confidentiality. The changed text removes this standard and extends the confidentiality period to 10 years. Cal. Civ. Code § 1643 states a contract must be interpreted to be 'reasonable, and capable of being carried into effect'. Removing the 'reasonable degree of care' standard, along with extending the term could create an unreasonable obligation, especially if the Confidential Information becomes obsolete during the 10-year period. This makes the contract more difficult to enforce and potentially contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: 5.8 Limitation of Liability IN NO EVENT WILL ANY EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INCIDENTAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, SPECIAL OR EXEMPLARY DAMAGES, WHETHER UNDER CONTRACT, TORT, WARRANTY, CONDIT ION OR OTHERWISE, ARISING IN ANY WAY IN RELATION TO THIS OR ANY OTHER RELATED AGREEMENT, WHETHER OR NOT SUCH PARTY HAD ADVANCE NOTICE OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.10 Authority to Sign The person signing on behalf of Participant hereby represents and warrants that he/she has the appropriate authorization to bind the Participant in this Agreement.

5.8 Limitation of Liability IN NO EVENT WILL ANY EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INCIDENTAL DAMAGES, WHETHER UNDER CONTRACT, TORT, WARRANTY, CONDIT ION OR OTHERWISE, ARISING IN ANY WAY IN RELATION TO THIS OR ANY OTHER RELATED AGREEMENT, WHETHER OR NOT SUCH PARTY HAD ADVANCE NOTICE OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.explanation: The clauses 'CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, SPECIAL OR EXEMPLARY DAMAGES' have been removed from the limitation of liability clause. This significantly reduces the scope of the liability protection, impacting potential legal remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specific disclaimers for consequential, indirect, special, or exemplary damages, the modified limitation of liability clause becomes less comprehensive. This could be interpreted as contradicting California Commercial Code § 2719, which allows for the limitation or exclusion of consequential damages unless the limitation is unconscionable. The altered clause, now narrower, might not provide sufficient protection against all types of damages, potentially rendering the limitation less effective and leading to unintended legal consequences if consequential damages are not explicitly excluded, they may be recoverable.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 7. Remedies [2701 - 2725] ( Chapter 7 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2719. (1) Subject to the provisions of subdivisions (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) The agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this division and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this division, as by limiting the buyer’s remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of nonconforming goods or parts; and (b) Resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this code. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is invalid unless it is proved that the limitation is not unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages where the loss is commercial is va
perturbation.justification: The original text limited liability for 'incidental, consequential, indirect, special or exemplary damages.' The changed text removes 'CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, SPECIAL OR EXEMPLARY DAMAGES,' thus narrowing the limitation. The Law Explanation states this could contradict California Commercial Code § 2719, because by removing the disclaimers for consequential, indirect, special, or exemplary damages, the modified limitation of liability clause becomes less comprehensive and may not provide sufficient protection against all types of damages. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2, Chapter 7, Section 2719 (3) states 'Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable.' The modified text, by removing these explicit exclusions, weakens the protection the original clause offered, and thus creates a contradiction with the spirit and potential application of section 2719 (3).
